Silicon Valley,
startups & Y Combinator

Where it came from, what a startup is, and how to start tonight.

The Map

Six stops, one through-line.

Jump in anywhere.

§01 · Origin Story

It wasn't the weather.

A professor, a few garages, a chunk of silicon. Click a year.

◂ ▸ or click the dots
§01 · The Trillion-Dollar Tree

The tree that grew a valley.

One bad boss, eight resignations — and the branches never stopped growing. Hit grow.

The Fairchildren: 92+ companies, ≈ $2 trillion.

Two of those branches were VC firms founded by Fairchild alumni — they funded the rest. Sometimes quitting your boss is a multi-trillion-dollar idea.

§02 · Reality Check

A startup is not a small business.

Same paperwork, different game. The difference is one word: growth.

A small business

  • A proven model — cafe, agency
  • Grows steadily, predictably
  • Often local, profitable early
  • Goal: a good living

A startup

  • Hunts a repeatable, scalable model
  • Built to grow fast, on purpose
  • Burns cash, bets on the future
  • Goal: swing for the fences

Paul Graham's whole definition: "A startup is a company designed to grow fast."

§03 · The Institution

Y Combinator: the startup factory.

Founded 2005. The valley's culture, made repeatable.

The standard deal

$125K for a fixed 7%, plus $375K on an uncapped SAFE that converts in your next round.

Same terms for everyone. No haggling.

01
Apply

A short form + a quick video call.

02
Batch

~11 weeks. Build, weekly goals, office hours.

03
Demo Day

Pitch the world's top investors.

$125K → fixed slice7.00%
$375K → uncapped SAFE3.75%
YC owns roughly10.75%

§03 · How It Began

A summer experiment in 2005.

Paul Graham had sold his startup Viaweb to Yahoo. With three friends, $200K, and frankly low expectations, he tried something weird: fund a whole batch of young hackers at once — about $6K per founder — mentor them over one summer, end with a demo day.

They called it the Summer Founders Program, run out of Cambridge, Massachusetts. The famous Tuesday dinners with guest speakers started right here.

"We didn't expect the startups we funded that summer to succeed."— Paul Graham, on the first batch

The name? "Y Combinator" is a function from computer science that builds other functions — a programmer's inside joke for a thing that makes startups.

Paul GrahamThe essayist

Lisp hacker, sold Viaweb to Yahoo, wrote the essays founders still quote.

Jessica LivingstonThe engine

Ran the program; later wrote "Founders at Work."

Robert MorrisThe legend

MIT professor — and author of the 1988 internet worm.

Trevor BlackwellThe roboticist

Built robots at Anybots; the technical muscle.

Eight tiny bets, summer 2005.

startups funded

RedditHuffman · Ohanian→ now public, $10B+

Faked ~99% of early posts with dummy accounts.

LooptSam Altman→ runs OpenAI

Yes — that Sam Altman was in batch one.

KikoKan · Shear→ became Twitch

A calendar first. Then they built Twitch.

InfogamiAaron Swartz→ merged into Reddit

The web prodigy who joined Reddit.

Summer
Founders
Program
· 2005 ·
the first 8

From eight scrappy experiments came Reddit, the CEO of OpenAI, and the founders of Twitch. The bar to get started was a short application.
Plus ClickFacts, TextPayMe, Memamp & Simmery — which quietly faded. That's the base rate.
Meet the real first batch →

§03 · Receipts

You already use their alumni.

Not theory — they're on your phone right now.

Worth hundreds of billions today. Every one started unproven — Airbnb couldn't make rent; Stripe was two brothers and an idea. Yes, even them.

War story · 2008

Broke and in debt, Airbnb's founders sold novelty cereal — Obama O's and Cap'n McCain's — and made ~$30K at $40 a box. The hustle, not the pitch, got them into YC: Paul Graham figured anyone who can sell a $4 box of cereal for $40 might just get strangers to sleep in strangers' homes.

§04 · The Ecosystem

YC is famous, not the only door.

Rule of thumb: communities are free and open now. Accelerators and VCs want you already building.

§04 · Doors Open Now

Live doors, this season.

Real programs taking applications now. Tap any to apply.

Status moves fast — always confirm dates on the program's own site.

§05 · Startup School

Same name. Three different things.

Only one is open to you tonight. Don't mix them up.

  Online course SF event · 2 days The YC batch
WhatSelf-paced courseLive conference~11-week program
CostFreeFree if picked$500K for 7%
EntryOpen sign-upApply · hand-pickedApply · very selective
WhenAnytime · ~7 wksEach summer4× a year

All three say "Y Combinator." Online extras, free: YC partner lessons · 100k+ co-founder matches · weekly check-ins.

The online course is an open sign-up — you literally cannot be rejected.
